validation.comparison_result: Results do not match. Expected revenue: 1230113636.0101, Got: 154016202.97 (8x error). Code uses hardcoded path to text CSV file instead of GenDB binary columnar storage.

Q6.notes: CRITICAL: Wrong results (8x off). Execution time is 357x slower than GenDB baseline (29.3ms). Code reads from text lineitem.tbl instead of GenDB binary columnar storage in /home/jl4492/GenDB/benchmarks/tpc-h/gendb/tpch_sf10.gendb/lineitem_*.col

Q6.bottleneck: Text CSV parsing instead of binary columnar mmap. File reading cost dominates (10.4 seconds for 60M rows).
Q6.root_cause: CRITICAL SEMANTIC ISSUE: Code hardcodes /home/jl4492/GenDB/benchmarks/tpc-h/data/sf10/lineitem.tbl (text format) instead of using GenDB's binary columnar storage. GenDB stores columns as: lineitem_l_quantity.col (239MB), lineitem_l_extendedprice.col (480MB), lineitem_l_discount.col (240MB), lineitem_l_shipdate.col (240MB). Parsing text with delimiter scanning and double/int conversions is ~357x slower than zero-copy mmap. Additionally, text parsing may be corrupting data (results are 8x off).

analysis.overall: Q6 is functionally broken (wrong results) and extremely slow (357x slower than GenDB baseline). Root cause: code uses hardcoded text CSV file path instead of the GenDB binary columnar storage directory passed as gendb_dir argument. This must be fixed before any performance optimizations can be meaningful. The text parsing also produces incorrect results, suggesting data corruption or field parsing bugs during CSV line iteration.

critical_fixes.issue: CRITICAL: Wrong results (expected 1230113636.0101, got 154016202.97). Code uses hardcoded text CSV file path instead of GenDB binary columnar storage.
critical_fixes.fix: Replace text CSV parsing with binary columnar mmap access. Load lineitem columns from GenDB directory: l_quantity (int32_t), l_extendedprice (int64_t), l_discount (int32_t), l_shipdate (int32_t). Use mmap + MADV_SEQUENTIAL for zero-copy scan.
critical_fixes.description: 1. Accept gendb_dir argument properly (currently hardcoded /home/jl4492/GenDB/benchmarks/tpc-h/data/sf10/lineitem.tbl). 2. Load binary column files: lineitem_l_quantity.col, lineitem_l_extendedprice.col, lineitem_l_discount.col, lineitem_l_shipdate.col from gendb_dir/lineitem/. 3. Use mmap(filename, O_RDONLY, PROT_READ, MAP_SHARED) to map each column. 4. Parse metadata.txt to get row count and encoding info (current code assumes row count but it's stored in metadata). 5. Implement proper encoding handling for all columns (storage_design.json shows l_extendedprice is int64_t in storage, l_discount is int32_t). 6. Filter predicates: l_discount BETWEEN 0.05 AND 0.07 (need to understand encoding: likely fixed-point multiplied by 100), l_quantity < 24, l_shipdate >= 1994-01-01 AND < 1995-01-01 (encoded as days since 1970). 7. Compute revenue as: SUM(l_extendedprice * l_discount / 10000.0) or similar depending on encoding. Remove all text parsing code.

performance_optimizations.description: After fixing semantic issue: Replace CSV parsing with mmap-based zero-copy access to GenDB's binary column files. Use madvise(MADV_SEQUENTIAL) to hint sequential scan pattern. This eliminates text parsing (CSV delimiter scanning, atoi/atod conversion) which currently dominates 357x slowdown. GenDB achieves 29.3ms via mmap; target is 30-50ms with single-threaded mmap access (GenDB likely uses 64-thread parallelism).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 357x (10401ms → ~30ms). Zero-copy mmap eliminates all parsing overhead. Sequential I/O hint ensures HDD prefetch works efficiently.

performance_optimizations.technique: Morsel-Driven Parallel Scan with Thread-Local Aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: After semantic fix: Current code attempts multi-threading (64 threads per hardware_concurrency) but morsel_size calculation is naive (50K rows minimum). With 60M rows and 64 cores, morsel size should be ~1M rows/core to amortize lock overhead. Implement: (1) Load column files with mmap. (2) Partition into 64 morsels of ~1M rows each. (3) Each thread: scan filter predicates, accumulate SUM locally in 64B-padded struct to avoid false sharing. (4) Final reduction sums thread-local results. Expected: 40-50x speedup (64 cores, near-linear). Current code doesn't use mmap so parallelism doesn't help much.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 40-50x over single-threaded (if mmap is used). From 30ms → 0.6-0.75ms with parallel binary scan on 64 cores.

performance_optimizations.description: After mmap + parallelism: Optimize filter evaluation. Current code uses sequential if-statements which cause branch mispredictions on random data. Implement: (1) Reorder filters by selectivity (discount 11%, quantity 48%, shipdate 15.7% → discount first as cheapest CPU-wise). (2) Use branch-free predicates: instead of `if (x < a || x > b) continue;`, compute `uint64_t mask = ((x >= 0.05) & (x <= 0.07)) & (qty < 24) & (date_in_range);` and skip with mask. (3) Consider SIMD vectorization: load 8×int32_t quantities into AVX2 register, compare all 8 in single instruction. (4) Keep filter chain compact to fit L1 cache (64B predicates × 3 = 192B, fits).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.5-2x. Eliminates branch mispredictions (on 64 cores with random data, significant). SIMD vectorization adds 1.3-1.5x more.

summary: CRITICAL FAILURE: Q6 produces wrong results (8x error) and runs 357x slower than GenDB baseline. Root cause is semantic: code hardcodes a path to text CSV file (/home/jl4492/GenDB/benchmarks/tpc-h/data/sf10/lineitem.tbl) instead of using the GenDB binary columnar storage directory argument. This must be fixed immediately by: (1) Loading binary column files via mmap from gendb_dir, (2) Understanding encoding (int32_t/int64_t raw storage per storage_design.json), (3) Implementing proper filter predicates on decoded values. ONLY after this semantic fix should performance optimizations be considered. The code architecture for parallelism is present (64 threads, thread-local sums, false-sharing prevention) but is ineffective when parsing slow text CSV. Once binary mmap is implemented, the current parallel structure will deliver ~40-50x speedup automatically. Additional optimizations (branch-free filtering, SIMD) can achieve another 1.5-2x, targeting <1ms execution.
